slides.headline: Two licences. One placement. Most composers only know about one.
fit_warnings: [01 hook.headline] copy (64 chars) exceeds slot budget (57 at 200px) — needs a rewrite

slides.body: If you've ever wondered why sync deals feel complicated — every placement requires two separate legal permissions. ↓

🔑 THE SYNC LICENCE
Permission to pair your composition with visual content.
Controlled by: the publisher (or you, if you self-publish).

🔑 THE MASTER LICENCE
Permission to use the specific recording.
Controlled by: whoever owns the master — often the label, unless you recorded independently.

If a label owns your master:
→ They share in every sync deal you ever make
→ A supervisor has to negotiate with two parties
→ That friction kills deals "One-stop" means you control both. Supervisors prioritise one-stop tracks.
Fewer calls. Faster deals. No surprises. → What the sync licence actually covers
→ What the master licence covers and who typically holds it
→ Why one-stop status changes everything
→ How to protect your position before you sign anything
→ What to do if a label already holds your master Save this before your next deal conversation. #MusicRights #SyncLicensing #AfricanComposers #SyncMaster #IndependentArtist
